---
title: "Tutorials"
---

These hands-on walkthroughs take you from raw data to a finished 3D
visualization. Each one exercises a different part of SciGraphs and maps every
step back to the panels documented in the [Panel Reference](../panels/overview.qmd).

| # | Tutorial | You will learn |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | [A combinatorial graph from a file](01-combinatorial-graph.qmd) | Import an edge list, lay it out, compute centrality and communities, and render. |
| 2 | [A geospatial flow map on a globe](02-geospatial-globe.qmd) | Geocode nodes, project onto a 3D globe, and draw geodesic arcs (à la the refugee-flows figure). |
| 3 | [An urban network on terrain](03-urban-terrain.qmd) | Download a street network with OSMnx, drape it onto DEM terrain, and add an amenity proximity graph (the Granada figure). |
| 4 | [A sparse matrix from SuiteSparse](04-sparse-matrix.qmd) | Import a benchmark matrix, lay it out, and colour by eigenvector centrality. |
| 5 | [A reproducible pipeline](05-reproducible-pipeline.qmd) | Capture a whole workflow in a declarative file and replay it with provenance. |

::: {.callout-tip}
If you are new to SciGraphs, start with tutorial 1, then read
[The end-to-end workflow](../guide/pipeline.qmd) to see how the stages fit
together.
:::

## Before you begin

- Install and enable SciGraphs (see [Installation](../guide/installation.qmd)).
- Open the 3D Viewport sidebar with `N` and locate the **SciGraphs**, **OSMnx**,
  and **City2Graph** tabs.
- For geospatial tutorials, ensure Blender has internet access.
